scenario_results.reason: The lane produced a beauty and wellness-specific handoff package with provider, consultation, consent, and ownership artifacts rather than generic scheduling notes.
Missing credential, supervision, guest-history, and consent prerequisites were treated as blockers instead of a routable handoff.
Duplicate ownership and conflicting schedule records were escalated with an owner map instead of dropped into informal coordination.
Injectable or device-service approval was rejected and routed to the correct clinical or medical-spa owner.
The lane refused to absorb provider assignment, treatment-note, and contraindication-clearance work.
Missing jurisdiction, owner-map, and supervision facts correctly triggered low-confidence blocked-dependency escalation.
Industry terms such as guest history, LMT intake, patch test, RN injector, and equipment binder were normalized into authoritative records and handoff states.
Generic spa-scheduling framing was rejected until provider-specific artifacts were supplied, proving the industry label was not treated as cosmetic.
Scope-of-practice and post-procedure questions were routed to licensed or clinical owners rather than answered in-lane.
Conflicting public and tenant supervision rules were escalated for human resolution instead of synthesized into a definitive instruction.
Closure was only confirmed after destination-owner proof and required artifact completeness were explicit.
Adverse-reaction and recent-procedure risk signals triggered licensed or safety escalation rather than unsupported continuation.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold while keeping the lane specific to licensed-provider coordination in beauty, wellness, and personal care settings.

Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all coordination-handoffs-and-routing abilities, and the cross-industry artifact-awareness ability required by the issue contract.
